Business Impact — Confusion Matrix
Best + Threshold model at t=0.32 on 20,000 test customers
9,116
True Positives
Churners caught — retention opportunity
796
False Negatives
Churners missed — $630K/yr invisible leak
7,538
False Positives
Loyal customers flagged — wasted spend
2,550
True Negatives
Loyal customers correctly identified
Key insight: The low threshold (0.32) deliberately trades precision for recall — we flag 7,538 false alarms to avoid missing 796 actual churners worth $630K/year in undetected revenue leak. The cost of a false alarm (an unnecessary retention offer) is far lower than a missed churner.
Model Leaderboard
All metrics comparison — baseline vs ensemble
Best + Threshold leads on F1 (0.6863) and Recall (0.9197). All ensembles share the same AUC-ROC (0.6851) — the difference is in threshold choice.
ROC Curves — All Models
True positive rate vs false positive rate across all thresholds
Ensemble models (bold lines) cluster at AUC≈0.685. Logistic Regression and KNN trail significantly. Random Forest shows the widest train-test gap (0.12) — most overfit.

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS
Understanding the Data
100,000 customers · 98 features after cleaning
Target Class Distribution
Churn vs Non-Churn balance in the full dataset
Near-balanced classes: The dataset has approximately equal churn/non-churn split in the training window, which is unusual for real telecom data (typically 5–15% churn). This makes accuracy a reliable metric here, but recall is still prioritised due to asymmetric business costs.
Missing Value Visualisation
Percentage of missing values per feature
Several features had non-trivial missing rates. All were imputed (median for numerical, mode for categorical) before modelling. 59 usage/revenue columns were winsorised at the 1st–99th percentile to handle outliers.
Key Numerical Features — Box Plots
Distribution of 10 key features including revenue, usage, and equipment age
rev_Mean ($10–$227) and hnd_price show wide IQR. eqpdays (equipment age) has a long right tail — some customers have very old devices, a known churn signal.
Numerical Features vs Churn — Side-by-Side Box Plots
How each key feature distributes differently between churners and non-churners
eqpdays and months show the clearest separation — longer-tenured customers with older equipment churn more. custcare_Mean (customer care calls) is elevated for churners, indicating service dissatisfaction.
Categorical Features — Bar Charts
Distribution of categorical variables
Churn Rate by Category
Which categories have the highest churn rates
Credit class code and area show meaningful churn rate variation across categories, confirming they carry predictive signal.
Correlation Heatmap — Top 20 Features
Pairwise correlations among the 20 features most correlated with churn
High intercorrelation among usage metrics (mou, comp, plcd, peak) is expected — they measure the same underlying behaviour (call activity). A variance threshold and correlation filter were applied to remove redundant features before modelling.
FEATURE ENGINEERING & SELECTION
Building Predictive Signals
4-stage pipeline: Variance → Correlation → Mutual Info → RF Importance
Mutual Information — Top 40 Features
Stage 3: Non-linear dependency with churn target
eqpdays, months, and revenue features consistently rank highest. MI captures non-linear relationships that Pearson correlation misses.
Random Forest Feature Importance — Top 25
Stage 4: Impurity-based importance from a 100-tree selector forest
eqpdays alone accounts for ~43% of split importance. This is the single most powerful churn predictor — customers with very old or very new equipment behave differently.
Best Model Feature Importance
Top features from the best tree-based model in the final pipeline
Top 10 features used by the best model: eqpdays (0.431) · months (0.245) · change_mou (0.077) · mou_Mean (0.066) · hnd_price (0.066) · totmrc_Mean (0.047) · crclscod (0.023) · totrev (0.011) · rev_per_minute (0.010) · avgqty (0.008)
BASELINE MODELS
Individual Model Results
5 baseline classifiers with confusion matrices and performance curves
Logistic Regression
F1: 0.5684 | Recall: 0.5692 | AUC: 0.6030
Weakest discriminator. Linear boundaries insufficient for this high-dimensional non-linear problem. Gap: 0.0041 (no overfitting).
Decision Tree
F1: 0.6507 | Recall: 0.7715 | AUC: 0.6344
Best single-model recall (0.7715) among baselines. Depth-5 limit prevents overfitting (gap: 0.003). The best baseline reference point for ensemble improvement.
K-Nearest Neighbours
F1: 0.5444 | Recall: 0.5277 | AUC: 0.5874
Worst overall performance. KNN suffers from the curse of dimensionality with 25 features. High train-test gap (0.10) indicates overfitting to training neighbours.
Random Forest
F1: 0.6353 | Recall: 0.6674 | AUC: 0.6729
Best AUC among baselines (0.6729) but highest overfitting gap (0.12). 200 trees at depth 12 memorise training patterns. Hyperparameter tuning helps significantly.
XGBoost
F1: 0.6287 | Recall: 0.6356 | AUC: 0.6826
RF Tuned
F1: 0.6473 | Recall: 0.7327 | AUC: 0.6556
RandomizedSearchCV reduced overfitting gap from 0.12 → 0.007. Recall improved substantially from 0.667 → 0.733.
XGBoost Tuned
F1: 0.6386 | Recall: 0.7245 | AUC: 0.6481
ENSEMBLE MODELS
LightGBM · Voting · Stacking
Three ensemble strategies — all sharing AUC-ROC = 0.6851
LightGBM (Tuned)
F1: 0.6378 | Recall: 0.6540 | AUC: 0.6851 | Gap: 0.0501
Gradient boosting with leaf-wise tree growth. Fastest training. Despite equal AUC to stacking, LightGBM's default threshold (0.5) gives lower recall — it is more conservative. The meta-learner in stacking assigns it weight +3.75, the highest of all base models.
Voting Ensemble
F1: 0.6372 | Recall: 0.6560 | AUC: 0.6838 | Gap: 0.0485
Soft voting averages the probability outputs of RF Tuned + XGBoost + LightGBM. Slightly lower AUC than stacking (0.6838 vs 0.6851) because equal weights are suboptimal — some models contribute more signal than others.
Stacking — Confusion Matrix & Meta-Learner Weights
F1: 0.6345 | Recall: 0.6466 | AUC: 0.6851 | Gap: 0.0557
Meta-learner coefficients (Logistic Regression on base-model probabilities):
LightGBM: +3.750 (most trusted) · XGBoost: +1.269 · RF Tuned: −0.096 · Decision Tree: −0.315 (actively discounted)

The meta-learner has empirically learned that LightGBM's calibrated probabilities are the most reliable signal. Decision Tree probabilities are noisy (poorly calibrated single trees) and are penalised.
THRESHOLD OPTIMISATION
Finding the Optimal Decision Boundary
Sweeping thresholds 0.10 → 0.90 to maximise F1 while pushing Recall above 72%
Interactive Threshold Explorer
0.32
0.10 (max recall)0.50 (default)0.90 (max precision)
F1 Score
0.6863
Recall
0.9197
Precision
0.5474
Churners Flagged
16,654
Status
✦ OPTIMAL
Threshold Optimisation Analysis
F1, Recall, Precision curves + Precision-Recall tradeoff (Stacking model)
Left chart: F1 (blue) peaks at t=0.32 (orange dashed line). Recall (red) falls monotonically — it is near 1.0 at t=0.10 and approaches 0 at t=0.90. Precision (green) rises inversely. The default t=0.50 (dotted line) sits where Precision and Recall are roughly equal (~0.62).

Right chart (Precision-Recall curve): Orange dot = optimal (t=0.32, Recall=0.92, Precision=0.55). Grey diamond = default (t=0.50, Recall=0.63, Precision=0.62). Moving from grey to orange: we gain +29pp recall at the cost of −7pp precision.

at t = 0.32 · +0.0518 improvement

REVENUE AT RISK ANALYSIS
Quantifying Financial Exposure
Using actual revenue columns: rev_Mean, totrev, adjrev, avgrev, avg3rev, avg6rev + 4 more

$950K

Undetected Annual Leak

Revenue leaving undetected
Customer Risk Segmentation by Churn Probability
Revenue exposure breakdown across Low / Medium / High risk tiers
⬛ High Risk
29.8%
5,960 cust.
⬛ Medium Risk
56.2%
11,244 cust.
⬛ Low Risk
14.0%
2,796 cust.
86% of the customer base is in medium or high risk territory. The high-risk segment (5,960 customers, churn probability >60%) should be the first intervention priority. At $57.10 avg monthly revenue, this segment represents ~$340K/month at risk — $4.08M/year if fully churned.
Revenue at Risk — Calculation Methods Explained
MethodFormulaMonthlyAnnualUse Case
Binary (Worst-Case) Σ rev_Mean [predicted=1] $950,888 $11.41M Executive budgeting, worst-case planning
Probability-Weighted Σ churn_proba × rev_Mean $568,510 $6.82M Financial modelling, ROI on retention spend
False Negative Leak Σ rev_Mean [actual=1, pred=0] $52,564 $630,774 Hidden revenue leaving undetected
Recoverable Revenue Binary − FN Leak $898,324 $10.78M Revenue the model gives us a chance to save
The $382K/month gap between binary ($950K) and probability-weighted ($568K) estimates reflects model uncertainty — not all flagged customers have equal churn probability. The probability-weighted figure is more accurate for financial planning; the binary figure is the upper bound for risk committees.
